provenance.source_corpus: Long-term functionality of woody debris structures for forest-floor small mammals on clearcuts

source_spans.text: Woody debris structures provided habitat on new clearcuts for M. gapperi at comparable or higher abundance than in uncut forest and 5.0 to 7.6 times higher than on dispersed sites in the first 5-year period.

Woody debris structures provided habitat on new clearcuts for M. gapperi at comparable or higher abundance than in uncut forest and 5.0 to 7.6 times higher than on dispersed sites in the first 5-year period.
Mean abundance of M. longicaudus was consistently higher (2.8 to 3.5 times) in piles and windrows than in sites with dispersed woody debris over the first 5-year period.
Mean abundance of P. maniculatus was similar among treatment sites and consistent over time.

Mean abundance of N. amoenus was significantly different among treatment sites with the piles and windrows at consistently higher (DMRT; P = 0.05) numbers (2.5 to 5.7 times on average) than the dispersed and forest sites in 2007 –2011

Mean abundance of total small mammals was consistently higher (1.8 to 2.4 times) in piles and windrows than dispersed or forest sites in the first 5-year period and this pattern was continued at 11 –12 years post-construction.
Our results did not support H1 as abundance, species richness, and diversity were increased or maintained in the debris structures over the 12-year period.
Our results did not support H1 as abundance, species richness, and diversity were increased or maintained in the debris structures over the 12-year period.
Reproduction and survival followed the pattern of abundance for the major species, and hence H2 was not supported.
Reproduction and survival followed the pattern of abundance for the major species, and hence H2 was not supported.
